---
title: "Generics in Java"
format: revealjs
code-font-size: "1.6em"
---

## The Problem: Same Logic, Different Types

Imagine you want to print the last element of an array. You write this:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
public static void printLast(int[] arr) {
    System.out.println(arr[arr.length - 1]);
}
```

Then your classmate asks: *"Can it work with Strings?"*

. . .

You write another version:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
public static void printLast(String[] arr) {
    System.out.println(arr[arr.length - 1]);
}
```

The logic is **identical**. Only the type changed.

---

## Method Overloading: A Quick Recap

**Method overloading** lets you define multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter types**.

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
public static int findMax(int[] arr) {
    int max = arr[0];
    for (int x : arr) if (x > max) max = x;
    return max;
}

public static double findMax(double[] arr) {
    double max = arr[0];
    for (double x : arr) if (x > max) max = x;
    return max;
}
```

Java picks the right version at **compile time** based on the argument type.

---

## Overloading: It Works… But It's Painful

::: {.incremental}
- ✅ Works correctly
- ❌ You are **copy-pasting the same logic** for every type
- ❌ If you find a bug, you must fix it in **every version**
- ❌ You can't support `String[]`, `Double[]`, `Student[]` without writing even more copies
- ❌ Code becomes harder to read and maintain
:::

. . .

> **Key Insight:** The logic doesn't depend on the type. Only the *placeholder* for the type changes.

---

## The Solution: Generic Methods

A **generic method** lets you write the logic **once** and let the caller supply the type.

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|"}
public static <T> void printLast(T[] arr) {
    System.out.println(arr[arr.length - 1]);
}
```

. . .

**Breaking it down:**

| Part | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `<T>` | Declares a **type parameter** — a placeholder for any type |
| Appears **before** the return type | Always placed between modifiers and return type |
| `T[]` | An array of *whatever type* the caller provides |

---

## Using a Generic Method

Java **infers** the type from what you pass in — no special syntax needed at the call site.

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Integer[] nums    = {10, 20, 30};
String[]  words   = {"apple", "banana", "cherry"};
Double[]  prices  = {1.99, 4.49, 2.75};

printLast(nums);    // prints 30
printLast(words);   // prints cherry
printLast(prices);  // prints 2.75
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
`T` must be an **object type** (like `Integer`, `String`, `Double`) — not a primitive (`int`, `double`). That's why we use `Integer[]` instead of `int[]`.
:::

---

## Generic findMax: One Method for All

Let's rewrite `findMax` generically. We need `T` to be **comparable**:

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|2|3|"}
public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T findMax(T[] arr) {
    T max = arr[0];
    for (T x : arr) if (x.compareTo(max) > 0) max = x;
    return max;
}
```

. . .

**`T extends Comparable<T>`** is a **bounded type parameter** — it means:

> *"T can be any type, as long as it knows how to compare itself to another T."*

`Integer`, `String`, `Double` all implement `Comparable` — so they all work.

---

## Calling findMax Generically

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Integer[] nums  = {3, 1, 7, 4};
String[]  names = {"Zara", "Alice", "Mia"};

System.out.println(findMax(nums));   // 7
System.out.println(findMax(names));  // Zara
```

. . .

Compare to the overloaded version — this is **one method** that handles everything correctly.

If you find a bug in the logic, you fix it **in one place**.

---

## From Generic Methods to Generic Classes

Sometimes the **type** is a property of the whole class, not just one method.

Think about a box that can hold *anything*:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
public class Box {
    private Object contents;

    public void put(Object item) { this.contents = item; }
    public Object get()          { return this.contents; }
}
```

. . .

Problem: `get()` returns `Object`, so you must **cast** every time:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Box b = new Box();
b.put("Hello");
String s = (String) b.get();  // cast required — easy to get wrong
```

---

## Generic Classes: The Fix

Declare the type parameter at the **class level** using `<T>`:

```{.java code-line-numbers="|1|2|3-4|"}
public class Box<T> {
    private T contents;
    public void put(T item)  { this.contents = item; }
    public T    get()        { return this.contents; }
}
```

. . .

Now each `Box` is locked to one type — no casting needed:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Box<String>  strBox = new Box<>();
Box<Integer> intBox = new Box<>();

strBox.put("Hello");
intBox.put(42);

String  s = strBox.get();  // no cast
Integer n = intBox.get();  // no cast
```

---

## Generic Classes: Multiple Type Parameters

A class can have **more than one** type parameter. Use different letters by convention:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
public class Pair<A, B> {
    private A first;
    private B second;

    public Pair(A first, B second) {
        this.first  = first;
        this.second = second;
    }

    public A getFirst()  { return first; }
    public B getSecond() { return second; }
}
```

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
Pair<String, Integer> grade = new Pair<>("Alice", 95);
System.out.println(grade.getFirst());   // Alice
System.out.println(grade.getSecond());  // 95
```

---

## You've Been Using Generic Classes All Along

::: {.incremental}
- `ArrayList<T>` — a list that holds objects of type T
:::

. . .

The diamond `<>` on the right lets Java infer the type from the left side:

```{.java code-line-numbers=""}
// These are all generic classes in the Java standard library:
ArrayList<String>  names   = new ArrayList<>();
ArrayList<Integer> scores  = new ArrayList<>();
```

You've been using generics the whole time — now you know how they work under the hood.

---

## When to Use Generics

| Situation | Use |
|-----------|-----|
| Same algorithm, different types | Generic **method** |
| A container/wrapper for any type | Generic **class** |
| You need type safety without casting | Either |
| Types are always the same (e.g., always `String`) | No generics needed |

. . .

**Rule of thumb:** If you catch yourself copy-pasting a method and only changing the type, it's time for a generic method.

---

## Summary

::: {.incremental}
- **Method overloading** lets multiple methods share a name — but you still write separate code for each type.
- **Generic methods** (`<T>`) write the logic once and work on any object type.
- **Bounded type parameters** (`<T extends Comparable<T>>`) add constraints so you can use specific methods on T.
- **Generic classes** (`Box<T>`, `Pair<A,B>`) let an entire class be parameterized by a type.
- You've already been using generic classes: `ArrayList<T>`. We will see a couple more in the next lecture.
:::
